skill.name: technical-research
skill.description: Technical spike and research investigation specialist. Use when exploring options for a technical decision, conducting timeboxed investigations, or evaluating technology choices.
skill.summary: Structure technical spikes, compare options, and document research recommendations.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 16 high-risk patterns, but manual review found they are false positives in markdown documentation. The skill contains a research workflow and a spike template, with no executable scripts, no network access, and no prompt injection text found.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Matches Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution hits are markdown code fences, inline formatting, or template artifact locations. They do not invoke a shell or define runnable code in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct inspection shows markdown delimiters and inline path formatting only. No executable command path or user-controlled shell invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Data Matches Are Placeholder Text
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM detections are caused by the placeholder token {{CODE_SAMPLE}} in the template. No Windows registry hive path, credential access, or sensitive file handling appears in the reviewed lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact context is a neutral template placeholder inside proof-of-concept sections. There is no semantic evidence of Windows SAM access.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Prose Tokens
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections are false positives from words such as Description and decision-oriented prose. The skill does not recommend DES, MD5, SHA1, or any cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed text contains generic research language and template headings. No cryptographic code, algorithm recommendation, or security downgrade is present.

content.value_statement: Technical choices often stall when research is informal or hard to compare. This skill provides a structured spike workflow for options, proof of concepts, risks, and recommendations.
actual_capabilities: Frames a technical investigation with objectives, questions, and success criteria.
Structures comparison of multiple implementation options.
Guides proof-of-concept documentation and findings capture.
Records risks, dependencies, effort estimates, and open questions.
Produces a recommendation with rationale and an implementation path.
limitations: It provides a documentation workflow, not automated benchmarking.
It does not validate external claims without separate research or testing.
It does not replace security, compliance, or architecture approval reviews.
Results depend on the quality of user-provided context and evidence.

use_cases.title: Compare Framework Options
use_cases.description: Evaluate two or three libraries with criteria, proof-of-concept notes, and a clear recommendation.

use_cases.title: Plan Architecture Decisions
use_cases.description: Turn an unclear technical choice into questions, constraints, risks, and an implementation path.

use_cases.title: Document Feasibility Research
use_cases.description: Capture timeboxed investigation results so product and engineering stakeholders can decide next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Spike
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a focused research task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a technical spike for this decision: [decision]. Include objective, key questions, success criteria, assumptions, and a timebox.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these options for [problem]: [option A], [option B], and [option C]. Use weighted criteria, risks, effort, and a recommendation.

prompt_templates.title: Review POC Findings
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert proof-of-concept notes into a decision-ready report.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use these proof-of-concept notes to complete a spike report: [notes]. Identify findings, pros, cons, risks, dependencies, and open questions.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare an Implementation Path
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning after research is complete.
prompt_templates.prompt: Given this selected approach and evidence, create an implementation path with phases, effort estimates, risk mitigations, and stakeholder-ready rationale.

output_examples.input: Evaluate whether to use a managed search service or run search infrastructure ourselves.
output_examples.output: A spike report with objective, constraints, option summaries, comparison criteria, risk table, recommendation, and phased implementation plan.

output_examples.input: Summarize POC results for three authentication libraries.
output: A findings section for each library.
A recommendation with risks, dependencies, and unanswered questions.

best_practices: Define decision criteria before comparing options.
Keep the investigation timeboxed and evidence focused.
Document risks and open questions even when the recommendation is clear.
anti_patterns: Using the spike to justify a decision already made.
Comparing options without runnable evidence or credible references.
Leaving out trade-offs, operational costs, or migration risks.

faq.question: What does this skill produce?
faq.answer: It produces structured technical spike and research documentation for engineering decisions.

faq.question: Does it run proof-of-concept code?
faq.answer: No. It helps document proof-of-concept work, but execution depends on the user and local tools.

faq.question: Can it compare more than three options?
faq.answer: Yes. The template starts with three options, but the structure can be extended.

faq.question: Is it useful for architecture decisions?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed for technology selection, feasibility studies, and architecture exploration.

faq.question: Does it make final decisions automatically?
faq.answer: No. It organizes evidence and recommendations so teams can make informed decisions.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
